questions.question: Which state was the first state to be renamed by government of India in 1950 after independence?
questions.explanation: After independence, Government of India changed the names of some the States and Union Territories. The United Provinces was the first State to have a new name. It was renamed Uttar Pradesh in 1950.

questions.question: In which year the Paper Currency Act was passed?
questions.explanation: The Paper Currency Act was passed in 1861. With this the British Government of India was conferred the monopoly to issue paper notes in India.

questions.question: Which one is the reason behind the suspension of Civil Disobedience Movement?

options.label: A
options.text: Violence broke out at Chauri Chaura

options.label: B
options.text: Gandhiji was arrested for five years

options.label: C
options.text: Government accepted the demands of Indian leaders

options.label: D
options.text: People were not supporting this movement

answer.label: A
answer.text: Violence broke out at Chauri Chaura

questions.explanation: The Chauri Chaura incident occurred at Chauri Chaura in the Gorakhpur district of the Uttar Pradesh on 5 February 1922. A large group of protesters, participating in the Non-cooperation movement, clashed with police, who opened fire. The incident led to the deaths of three civilians and 22 policemen. Mahatma Gandhi, who was strictly against violence, halted the non co-operation movement on the national level on 12 February 1922, as a direct result of this incident.

questions.question: What is true of Indian National Congress during World War II?

options.label: A
options.text: It demanded for its co-operation with the British that a provincial national government may be set up at the centre

options.label: B
options.text: It extended full support to the Indian National Army to oust the British from India

options.label: C
options.text: It decided to extend full support to the allied nations

options.label: D
options.text: It worked for the defeat of the allied nations

answer.label: A
answer.text: It demanded for its co-operation with the British that a provincial national government may be set up at the centre

questions.explanation: The Congress refused to let the government use Indian men, money and material in the war. They could be used only on the condition of granting the right of self-determination to the Indians. But the government refused to accept this and the Congress ministries resigned.

questions.question: Why did Gandhiji withdraw the Non- Cooperation Movement?

options.label: C
options.text: Because he was ordered to do so by the Britishers

options.label: D
options.text: Because he did not like to continue the movement

questions.explanation: The Non-cooperation movement was withdrawn because of the Chauri Chaura incident. Although he had stopped the national revolt single-handedly, on 10 March 1922, Gandhi was arrested. But many historians and Indian leaders of the time also defended Gandhi’s judgement.

questions.question: In which year, Mahatma Gandhi carried out Dandi March? (UPSC Prelims 1985)
questions.explanation: On 12th March 1930, Mahatma Gandhi started a march from Sabarmati Ashram in Ahmadabad to the sea shore at Dandi. So that is exactly what Gandhi decided to do; pick up salt from the seashore at Dandi and start the Civil Disobedience Satyagraha which was a protest against the policies of British empire. The march began on 12th March and ended on 5th April, 1930.

questions.question: Match the columns correctly: A. Madan Mohan Malviya 1. Ghadar Movement B. Sohan Singh Bhakna 2. Swatantra Party C. Annie Besant 3. Hindu Mahasabha D. Rajagopalachari 4. Theosophical Society of India (UPSC Prelims 1988)
questions.explanation: The Ghadar Party was an Indian revolutionary organization primarily founded by Indians. The party was multi-ethnic and had Sikh, Hindu and Muslim leaders. The party was headquartered in San Francisco, United States. Key members included Bhai Parmanand, Sohan Singh Bhakna. The Swatantra Party was an Indian classical liberal political party that existed from 1959 to 1974. It was founded by C. Rajagopalachari in reaction to what he felt was the Jawaharlal Nehru-dominated Indian National Congress’s increasingly socialist and statist outlook. The Hindu Mahasabha was founded in 1914 in Amritsar by Pandit Madan Mohan Malaviya. The Theosophical Society was founded by Madame Blavatsky and Col. Olcott in 1875 in New York. It was only in 1879, that this ideology gained its roots in the Indian culture and Society. The movement was popularized by Annie Besant in India.

questions.question: Who first calculated India’s per capita income as Rs. 20 in 1867-68? (UPSC Prelims 2000)
questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naoroji, known as the ‘Grand Old Man of India,’ first estimated India’s per capita income at Rs. 20 for 1867-68. His pioneering calculations highlighted economic exploitation under British rule and laid the foundation for later economic nationalist thought.

questions.question: Who among the following leaders proposed to adopt Complete Independence as the goal of the Congress in the Ahmedabad session of 1920? (UPSC Prelims 2001)
questions.explanation: Hasrat Mohani was an Indian activist, Freedom Fighter in the Indian independence movement, founder leader of Indian Communist Party and a noted poet of the Urdu language. He coined the notable slogan Inquilab Zindabad in 1921.

questions.question: Who was the Vice President of the executive council formed during the interim government in 1946?
questions.explanation: The formation of the interim government was announced on 24th August, 1946. Viceroy was to be the President of the executive council. And Jawaharlal Nehru was designated the Vice President of the executive council.
